one_line_takeaway: By explicitly attacking the router-expert coupling in video Mixture-of-Experts models (steering perturbed inputs toward the router's weakest expert), the paper's J-TLGA attack collapses adversarially-trained video MoE robust accuracy from ~40% (under naive PGD-router attacks) to as low as 2.5%, and the matching hierarchical J-TLAT defense recovers substantial robustness (e.g. 33.96% vs. 0-9.78% for prior defenses under the strongest attack) while cutting inference GFLOPs by more than 60% relative to dense adversarially-trained models.
key_finding: On UCF-101 with a 3D-ResNet-18-expert MoE trained by standard adversarial training (AT), conventional router-only attack PGD-R only degrades robust accuracy to 42.20% (eps=8/255) down to 39.34% (eps=14/255), i.e. the router looks deceptively robust to a naive attack. The paper's TLA-R attack drops this to 23.52%->21.65%, and TLGA-R (steering to the weakest expert) drops it further to 18.13%->15.82% -- described in the text as ~24% stronger than PGD-R. Expert-only attacks: PGD-E 31.98%->22.97% vs TLA-E 28.11%->19.89%. The joint attacks are far more destructive: J-TLA reduces robust accuracy to 7.03%(eps=8/255)->4.73%(eps=14/255), and J-TLGA (the strongest attack) reduces it to only 4.95%->2.54%. Against other defense baselines under J-TLGA (eps=8/255): AAT-M collapses to 0.00% and OUD-M falls to 2.64%. On the defense side (Table 2, UCF-101, 3D ResNet backbone): clean accuracy is AT-S 49.89%, AT-D 54.51%, AT-M 49.23%, OUD-M 51.67%, AAT-M 49.67%, TLAT 51.65%, J-TLAT 54.29%. Under PGD (eps=8/255) robust accuracy is AT-S 23.85%, AT-D 24.84%, AT-M 19.23%, OUD-M 15.16%, AAT-M 23.08%, TLAT 30.22%, J-TLAT 36.37% (best). Under the strongest attack J-TLGA (eps=8/255), J-TLAT reaches 33.96% robust accuracy versus AT-M 6.15%, OUD-M 2.64%, AAT-M 0.00%, TLAT 9.78% -- the text summarizes this as roughly a 34% robust-accuracy improvement over AAT-M under the strongest joint attack. The paper's own Lipschitz-constant metric (Lips-J, lower = more robust by their claim) is 596.0 for AT-M, 1474 for OUD-M, 1157 for AAT-M, 208.0 for TLAT, and 0.823/2.343 (Lips-R/Lips-J respectively) for J-TLAT, the lowest of all methods. Compute: J-TLAT uses 1.831 GFLOPs vs AT-D's (dense) 4.790 GFLOPs, i.e. >60% inference-cost reduction, matching the abstract's claim. Ablations (Fig. 3/5) show TLA outperforms plain PGD, Lipschitz-only (LIP), and temporal-only (T-PGD) attack variants as the internal attack used inside J-TLAT training (e.g., 33.63% robust accuracy under PGD, 30.33% under J-TLGA), and J-TLAT's regularization outperforms JS/COS/KL/NR/GR alternatives (36.59%/33.63%/30.33% across three attacks). Table 3 shows J-TLGA also has the best (lowest remaining accuracy for the victim) black-box transfer performance across 3D ResNet/Slowfast/R(2+1)D source-target pairs among PGD/FGSM/TT/J-TLGA.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is a family of component-aware adversarial attacks and a matching adversarial-training defense for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) video-understanding models. Specifically: (1) Temporal Lipschitz-Guided Attacks (TLGA) — attacks that target the router alone (TLA-R / TLGA-R) or an expert alone (TLA-E), built from a differentiable local-Lipschitz-constant loss combined with a temporal, gradient-momentum-based adaptive step size per frame; (2) Joint Temporal Lipschitz-Guided Attacks (J-TLA / J-TLGA), which simultaneously perturb the router (to steer routing toward the router's least-confident/'weakest' expert) and the whole MoE model, exposing what the authors call the 'Achilles' Heel' (collaborative weakness) of MoE; and (3) Joint Temporal Lipschitz Adversarial Training (J-TLAT), a 3-step-per-epoch hierarchical defense that adversarially trains the router for output consistency, then adversarially trains the top-2 weakest experts identified via TLGA-R, then adversarially trains the whole model end-to-end. A supporting theoretical Lipschitz upper-bound derivation for MoE is placed in the appendix (not verified here since only the first ~14 pages were extracted).
threat_model: White-box adversary with full knowledge of the video MoE's architecture, parameters, and gradients (explicitly stated: 'the subsequent methods are all configured as white-box attacks'). The attacker crafts an L-infinity-bounded additive perturbation delta (budget epsilon in {8/255, 10/255, 12/255, 14/255}) on an input video clip x in R^(C x T x H x W) to cause misclassification, either by (a) attacking the router only to flip/collapse routing decisions, (b) attacking one or more experts only, or (c) jointly attacking router and experts/whole model. The authors justify the white-box choice by arguing a defense robust to white-box attacks should also resist weaker gray-/black-box attacks, and separately report black-box transfer-attack results (Table 3) as a secondary check, not the primary threat model. This is an adversarial-robustness / model-integrity threat model (safety-critical video applications such as autonomous driving and surveillance are cited as motivating deployment contexts), not a data-security, privacy, or supply-chain threat model.
limitations: (1) The primary threat model is exclusively white-box (full access to router/expert parameters and gradients); black-box results (Table 3) are presented only as a secondary transfer-attack check, not as the main evaluation, so real-world applicability to opaque, deployed video MoE systems is unproven. (3) Baselines OUD-M and AAT-M are adaptations of image/CNN-era or black-box-only defenses (OUDefend, AAT) re-purposed for video MoE by the authors, which may not represent the strongest possible competing defenses for this exact setting. (4) The method assumes a differentiable, white-box-accessible router and a small, fixed number of experts (4); it is unclear how the approach scales to production-style MoE (e.g., large expert counts, Top-k>1 gating, or non-visual/token-level routers). (5) The 'Lipschitz constant' robustness metric (Lips-R/Lips-J) used to argue J-TLAT's superiority is the authors' own finite-difference proxy, not an independently certified bound, and its correlation with the reported accuracy numbers, while directionally consistent in the tables, is not independently validated against a certified-robustness method in the paper.

datasets_used: HMDB-51 (stated in experimental setup; results not visible in extracted pages, likely in appendix)
quantitative_results: PGD-R robust accuracy on AT-trained MoE (UCF-101, 3D ResNet-18): 42.20% (eps=8/255) down to 39.34% (eps=14/255)
TLGA-R robust accuracy: 18.13% (eps=8/255) down to 15.82% (eps=14/255), ~24% stronger than PGD-R per the text
PGD-E robust accuracy: 31.98% (eps=8/255) down to 22.97% (eps=14/255); TLA-E: 28.11% down to 19.89%
J-TLGA (strongest joint attack) robust accuracy: 4.95% (eps=8/255) down to 2.54% (eps=14/255)
Under J-TLGA eps=8/255, OUD-M drops to 2.64% and AAT-M collapses to 0.00%
Table 2 clean accuracy: AT-S 49.89%, AT-D 54.51%, AT-M 49.23%, OUD-M 51.67%, AAT-M 49.67%, TLAT 51.65%, J-TLAT 54.29%
Table 2 PGD (eps=8/255) robust accuracy: AT-S 23.85%, AT-D 24.84%, AT-M 19.23%, OUD-M 15.16%, AAT-M 23.08%, TLAT 30.22%, J-TLAT 36.37%
Under J-TLGA eps=8/255 (Lips-J table): AT-M 6.15%, OUD-M 2.64%, AAT-M 0.00%, TLAT 9.78%, J-TLAT 33.96% (~34% gain over AAT-M per text)
Lipschitz constant Lips-J (lower=more robust per authors): AT-M 596.0, OUD-M 1474, AAT-M 1157, TLAT 208.0, J-TLAT 2.343
Compute: J-TLAT 1.831 GFLOPs vs AT-D (dense) 4.790 GFLOPs, i.e. >60% inference-cost reduction
Ablation (Fig.3/5): TLA achieves 33.63% robust accuracy under PGD (5.5% gain over conventional PGD) and 30.33% under J-TLGA, outperforming LIP and T-PGD variants
J-TLAT regularization ablation: 36.59% / 33.63% / 30.33% robust accuracy across three attacks, best among JS/COS/KL/NR/GR alternatives
baselines_compared: TT / temporal-translation video attack (Wei et al. 2022)
OUD-M / OUDefend (Lo et al. 2021, restoration-network defense)
AAT-M / AAT (Kinfu & Vidal 2022, curriculum+adaptive adversarial training)
